summary: Stancil argues that writers and data professionals inevitably tailor what they say to the audience they want to impress, often defaulting to an insular circle of industry peers and influencers. This inward focus distorts priorities in the data community—conference talk, tool debates, and discourse drift toward self-referential validation rather than serving end users. The remedy is to deliberately broaden the audience we write and speak for, seeking respect from people outside the data bubble.
money_quotes: We have to bring other people back into our conversations.
The point I’d hoped to make was that it’s not the content of what we talk about that matters, but who we make that content for.
We can build companies, careers, and reputations on each other alone.

For people who talk on the internet, our religion is the opinions of other people who talk on the internet—particularly those who are more successful, who make more money, who are more widely followed, or who simply talk and write better than we do.
The solution, then, is both easy and incredibly difficult: We have to bring other people back into our conversations.
key_insight: The real problem isn’t what the data community talks about but the narrow audience it talks to, and widening that audience is the only way to regain perspective on what actually matters.
